frameworks.name: attractor dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Bistable recurrent E-I network with two attractor states underlying UP-DOWN transitions

frameworks.name: spiking network model
frameworks.one_line: Mechanistic spiking model with slow synaptic adaptation gating dwell-time distribution

frameworks.name: neural oscillation
frameworks.one_line: Periodic external-drive oscillator account argued against as origin of UP-DOWN cycling

core_question: Do cortical UP-DOWN transitions arise from bistability of a recurrent excitatory-inhibitory network rather than from periodic external drive?
key_assumptions: Cortical circuit is a recurrent excitatory-inhibitory network
Slow synaptic adaptation acts on a timescale relevant to state dwell times
Spontaneous activity can be modeled as transitions between discrete attractor states
manipulations: Brief external stimuli delivered to probe stimulus-locked UP-state onsets
Slow synaptic adaptation strength varied in the spiking-network model
Compared spontaneous vs stimulus-triggered transition statistics
key_findings: Two model attractor states match observed UP-state firing rate and DOWN-state silence
Slow synaptic adaptation gates the UP/DOWN dwell-time distribution
Brief stimuli trigger UP-state onsets with stimulus-locked timing
interpretations: UP-DOWN transitions reflect attractor bistability rather than a periodic oscillation
Cortical spontaneous activity is a signature of bistable network dynamics
